claims.title: State-then-plan sequential (non-atomic) writes
claims.detail: cmd.py:180 saves state via save_state_or_exit before _update_living_plan_after_resolve (line 182) updates the plan. The two writes are sequential and non-transactional. A crash between them leaves state updated (issues resolved) while plan still references the old IDs. Each individual write IS atomic (safe_write_text uses temp+rename), so no partial-write corruption can occur; the risk is purely a state/plan desync window.

claims.title: Plan save failure is swallowed with only a warning
claims.detail: save_plan raises OSError on failure. PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS includes OSError, so the failure IS caught (not truly swallowed) — but prior to this fix only a user-facing warning was printed with no recovery attempt. After this fix, the except block now reloads the plan from disk, runs reconcile_plan_after_scan to supersede newly-resolved IDs, and tries a second save, before falling back to the warning.

claims.title: Reconciliation direction makes desync worse / goes wrong direction
claims.detail: reconcile_plan_after_scan (engine/_plan/reconcile.py:143) uses state as authoritative: it iterates IDs referenced in the plan and for each one calls _is_issue_alive(state, fid). Issues that are no longer open in state are moved to plan.superseded. So reconciliation goes state→plan (state wins), and on the next scan after a failed plan save, the desync IS self-healed. The system does not re-open issues or corrupt state from a stale plan.

fix_description: In _update_living_plan_after_resolve (cmd.py), wrapped save_plan in an inner try/except. On save failure, reloads plan from disk, applies reconcile_plan_after_scan(fresh_plan, state) to supersede the now-resolved IDs, then retries save_plan. Falls back to user warning only if the recovery also fails. Adds 'state' parameter to the function; call site in cmd_resolve updated accordingly.
notes: Real gap confirmed: the state+plan write pair is non-transactional and a plan-save failure left no recovery path. Submission overstates severity ('corruption') since each individual write is atomic via temp+rename and the system self-heals on the next scan. The reconciliation-direction claim is factually wrong — reconcile is state-authoritative and would have healed the desync on the next scan regardless.
claim_restated: cmd_resolve performs two sequential writes — save_state_or_exit() at cmd.py:180 persists resolved issue state before _update_living_plan_after_resolve() at cmd.py:182 calls save_plan(). Each write is individually atomic via safe_write_text (os.replace temp+rename), but the pair is non-transactional: a crash or SIGKILL between them leaves state.json updated (issues marked resolved/wontfix) while plan.json still lists those issue IDs in queue_order. Additionally, a plan-save failure inside _update_living_plan_after_resolve was caught by the outer except PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS block but the catch body only printed a warning — no retry, no rollback, no recovery path — leaving plan and state silently out of sync until the next full scan.
why_poor_engineering: A two-phase persistence operation (state then plan) with no rollback, no retry, and no transactional guarantee is a reliability defect in a tool whose correctness depends on state/plan consistency. The exception handler for plan-save failure existed and caught the error (PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS includes OSError), but responded with only a user-facing warning — no recovery action. This left the tool in a user-visible inconsistent state (plan shows tasks already marked resolved) until the next full scan ran reconcile_plan_after_scan. Forcing the user to rely on an implicit future scan for recovery — with no guarantee of when that scan runs — is poor engineering for a persistence-critical code path.

why_missed.each_write_is_atomic: safe_write_text uses os.replace for atomic temp+rename, so neither file is ever partially written. The atomicity of each individual write masked the non-atomicity of the write pair — the persistence code appeared safe because no file could be left in a corrupt half-written state.
why_missed.exception_was_caught: The OSError from save_plan was caught by except PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS (which includes OSError). A reviewer seeing a caught exception might assume the error is handled — the code does respond to the failure, just inadequately (warning only, no recovery action).
why_missed.reconcile_heals_desync: reconcile_plan_after_scan would self-heal the desync on the next scan, creating the impression that any inconsistency is temporary and benign. The existence of an implicit recovery path (even one requiring a future user action) reduced the perceived severity of the missing immediate recovery.
